frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment: Achha hai beta, shuru shuru mein sab equal-equal bolte hain, par ghar rota se nahi chalta, zimmedari se chalta hai. Aadmi do din help kar de toh theek, lekin aakhir mein ghar sambhalna aur dekhna toh aurat ko hi padta hai, tabhi system tikta hai. Tum dekh lena, office ke saath itna hisaab-kitab waale kaam zyada din nahi chalte.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it normalizes domestic labor as a woman's natural responsibility while framing male participation as temporary 'help.' The surface manifestation is a practical-sounding justification for a gendered division of chores rather than explicit hostility toward women.
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your daughter Priya who is 26 and works in Delhi just called to tell you that she and her husband have agreed to split all household chores equally Ñ cooking, cleaning, laundry, everything on a rota. Priya sounds pleased about it. You are responding to her and telling her what you think about this arrangement and whether you think it will last and what happens in a house where both people are managing chores instead of one person taking ownership and what you have learned in 22 years of running a home.
